question:
5. If \( f(x) \) is a real-valued function defined for \( 0<x<1 \), then the formula \( f(x)=o(x) \) is an abbreviation for the statement that
\[
\frac{f(x)}{x} \rightarrow 0 \quad \text { as } x \rightarrow 0
\]

Keeping this in mind, prove the following: if
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=0 \text { and } f(x)-f\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)=o(x)
\]
then \( f(x)=o(x) \).
solution:
Solution. Let \( \epsilon>0 \) be given. Choose \( \delta \) so that for all \( x \) satisfying \( 0<x<\delta \)
\[
\left|\frac{1}{x}[f(x)-f(x / 2)]\right|<\frac{1}{2} \epsilon .
\]

Now fix \( y, 0<y<\delta \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{l} 
f(y)=\left[f(y)-f\left(\frac{y}{2}\right)\right]+\left[f\left(\frac{y}{2}\right)-f\left(\frac{y}{4}\right)\right] \\
+\cdots+\left[f\left(\frac{y}{2^{n-1}}\right)-f\left(\frac{y}{2^{n}}\right)\right]+f\left(\frac{y}{2^{n}}\right)
\end{array}
\]

So
\[
\begin{array}{l}
|f(y)| \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lvert\, f\left(\frac{y}{2^{i-1}}\right)\right.-f\left(\frac{y}{2^{i}}\right)\left|+\left|f\left(\frac{y}{2^{n}}\right)\right|\right. \\
\leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{y}{2^{i}} \epsilon+\left|f\left(\frac{y}{2^{n}}\right)\right|=y \epsilon\left(1-\frac{1}{2^{n}}\right)+\left|f\left(\frac{y}{2^{n}}\right)\right|
\end{array}
\]
using (1).
Letting \( n \rightarrow \infty \) we have
\[
|f(y)| \leq \epsilon y
\]
since \( f(x)-0 \) as \( x-0 \).
Thus we have proved: For all \( \epsilon>0 \), there is a \( \delta>0 \) such that \( |f(y) / y| \) \( \leq \epsilon \) provided \( 0<y<\cdot \delta \). But, by definition, this is
\[
f(x)=o(x) .
\]

descriptive_long.question:
5. If \( function(variablex) \) is a real-valued function defined for \( 0<variablex<1 \), then the formula \( function(variablex)=o(variablex) \) is an abbreviation for the statement that
\[
\frac{function(variablex)}{variablex} \rightarrow 0 \quad \text { as } variablex \rightarrow 0
\]

Keeping this in mind, prove the following: if
\[
\lim _{variablex \rightarrow 0} function(variablex)=0 \text { and } function(variablex)-function\left(\frac{variablex}{2}\right)=o(variablex)
\]
then \( function(variablex)=o(variablex) \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let \( smallconst>0 \) be given. Choose \( smallrange \) so that for all \( variablex \) satisfying \( 0<variablex<smallrange \)
\[
\left|\frac{1}{variablex}[function(variablex)-function(variablex / 2)]\right|<\frac{1}{2} smallconst .
\]

Now fix \( variabley, 0<variabley<smallrange \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{l} 
function(variabley)=\left[function(variabley)-function\left(\frac{variabley}{2}\right)\right]+\left[function\left(\frac{variabley}{2}\right)-function\left(\frac{variabley}{4}\right)\right] \\
+\cdots+\left[function\left(\frac{variabley}{2^{indexnn-1}}\right)-function\left(\frac{variabley}{2^{indexnn}}\right)\right]+function\left(\frac{variabley}{2^{indexnn}}\right)
\end{array}
\]

So
\[
\begin{array}{l}
|function(variabley)| \leq \sum_{indexii=1}^{indexnn} \left\lvert\, function\left(\frac{variabley}{2^{indexii-1}}\right)\right.-function\left(\frac{variabley}{2^{indexii}}\right)\left|+\left|function\left(\frac{variabley}{2^{indexnn}}\right)\right|\right. \\
\leq \sum_{indexii=1}^{indexnn} \frac{variabley}{2^{indexii}} smallconst+\left|function\left(\frac{variabley}{2^{indexnn}}\right)\right|=variabley smallconst\left(1-\frac{1}{2^{indexnn}}\right)+\left|function\left(\frac{variabley}{2^{indexnn}}\right)\right|
\end{array}
\]
using (1).
Letting \( indexnn \rightarrow \infty \) we have
\[
|function(variabley)| \leq smallconst variabley
\]
since \( function(variablex)\to0 \) as \( variablex\to0 \).
Thus we have proved: For all \( smallconst>0 \), there is a \( smallrange>0 \) such that \( |function(variabley) / variabley| \leq smallconst \) provided \( 0<variabley<smallrange \). But, by definition, this is
\[
function(variablex)=o(variablex) .
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
5. If \( meadowspur(gazeboair) \) is a real-valued function defined for \( 0<gazeboair<1 \), then the formula \( meadowspur(gazeboair)=o(gazeboair) \) is an abbreviation for the statement that
\[
\frac{meadowspur(gazeboair)}{gazeboair} \rightarrow 0 \quad \text { as } gazeboair \rightarrow 0
\]

Keeping this in mind, prove the following: if
\[
\lim _{gazeboair \rightarrow 0} meadowspur(gazeboair)=0 \text { and } meadowspur(gazeboair)-meadowspur\left(\frac{gazeboair}{2}\right)=o(gazeboair)
\]
then \( meadowspur(gazeboair)=o(gazeboair) \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let \( sandpiper>0 \) be given. Choose \( kingfisher \) so that for all \( gazeboair \) satisfying \( 0<gazeboair<kingfisher \)
\[
\left|\frac{1}{gazeboair}[meadowspur(gazeboair)-meadowspur(gazeboair / 2)]\right|<\frac{1}{2} sandpiper .
\]

Now fix \( lanternglow, 0<lanternglow<kingfisher \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{l} 
meadowspur(lanternglow)=\left[meadowspur(lanternglow)-meadowspur\left(\frac{lanternglow}{2}\right)\right]+\left[meadowspur\left(\frac{lanternglow}{2}\right)-meadowspur\left(\frac{lanternglow}{4}\right)\right] \\
+\cdots+\left[meadowspur\left(\frac{lanternglow}{2^{orchardmix-1}}\right)-meadowspur\left(\frac{lanternglow}{2^{orchardmix}}\right)\right]+meadowspur\left(\frac{lanternglow}{2^{orchardmix}}\right)
\end{array}
\]

So
\[
\begin{array}{l}
|meadowspur(lanternglow)| \leq \sum_{planktonic=1}^{orchardmix} \left\lvert\, meadowspur\left(\frac{lanternglow}{2^{planktonic-1}}\right)\right.-meadowspur\left(\frac{lanternglow}{2^{planktonic}}\right)\left|+\left|meadowspur\left(\frac{lanternglow}{2^{orchardmix}}\right)\right|\right. \\
\leq \sum_{planktonic=1}^{orchardmix} \frac{lanternglow}{2^{planktonic}} sandpiper+\left|meadowspur\left(\frac{lanternglow}{2^{orchardmix}}\right)\right|=lanternglow sandpiper\left(1-\frac{1}{2^{orchardmix}}\right)+\left|meadowspur\left(\frac{lanternglow}{2^{orchardmix}}\right)\right|
\end{array}
\]
using (1).
Letting \( orchardmix \rightarrow \infty \) we have
\[
|meadowspur(lanternglow)| \leq sandpiper lanternglow
\]
since \( meadowspur(gazeboair)\to 0 \) as \( gazeboair\to 0 \).
Thus we have proved: For all \( sandpiper>0 \), there is a \( kingfisher>0 \) such that \( |meadowspur(lanternglow) / lanternglow| \) \( \leq sandpiper \) provided \( 0<lanternglow<\cdot kingfisher \). But, by definition, this is
\[
meadowspur(gazeboair)=o(gazeboair) .
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
5. If \( malfunction(vastvalue) \) is a real-valued function defined for \( 0<vastvalue<1 \), then the formula \( malfunction(vastvalue)=o(vastvalue) \) is an abbreviation for the statement that
\[
\frac{malfunction(vastvalue)}{vastvalue} \rightarrow 0 \quad \text { as } vastvalue \rightarrow 0
\]

Keeping this in mind, prove the following: if
\[
\lim _{vastvalue \rightarrow 0} malfunction(vastvalue)=0 \text { and } malfunction(vastvalue)-malfunction\left(\frac{vastvalue}{2}\right)=o(vastvalue)
\]
then \( malfunction(vastvalue)=o(vastvalue) \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let \( enormity>0 \) be given. Choose \( distance \) so that for all \( vastvalue \) satisfying \( 0<vastvalue<distance \)
\[
\left|\frac{1}{vastvalue}[malfunction(vastvalue)-malfunction(vastvalue / 2)]\right|<\frac{1}{2} enormity .
\]

Now fix \( colossal, 0<colossal<distance \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{l} 
malfunction(colossal)=\left[malfunction(colossal)-malfunction\left(\frac{colossal}{2}\right)\right]+\left[malfunction\left(\frac{colossal}{2}\right)-malfunction\left(\frac{colossal}{4}\right)\right] \\
+\cdots+\left[malfunction\left(\frac{colossal}{2^{minuscule-1}}\right)-malfunction\left(\frac{colossal}{2^{minuscule}}\right)\right]+malfunction\left(\frac{colossal}{2^{minuscule}}\right)
\end{array}
\]

So
\[
\begin{array}{l}
|malfunction(colossal)| \leq \sum_{gigantic=1}^{minuscule} \left\lvert\, malfunction\left(\frac{colossal}{2^{gigantic-1}}\right)\right.-malfunction\left(\frac{colossal}{2^{gigantic}}\right)\left|+\left|malfunction\left(\frac{colossal}{2^{minuscule}}\right)\right|\right. \\
\leq \sum_{gigantic=1}^{minuscule} \frac{colossal}{2^{gigantic}} enormity+\left|malfunction\left(\frac{colossal}{2^{minuscule}}\right)\right|=colossal\ enormity\left(1-\frac{1}{2^{minuscule}}\right)+\left|malfunction\left(\frac{colossal}{2^{minuscule}}\right)\right|
\end{array}
\]
using (1).
Letting \( minuscule \rightarrow \infty \) we have
\[
|malfunction(colossal)| \leq enormity\ colossal
\]
since \( malfunction(vastvalue)-0 \) as \( vastvalue-0 \).
Thus we have proved: For all \( enormity>0 \), there is a \( distance>0 \) such that \( |malfunction(colossal) / colossal| \leq enormity \) provided \( 0<colossal<\cdot distance \). But, by definition, this is
\[
malfunction(vastvalue)=o(vastvalue) .
\]

garbled_string.question:
5. If \( bnchwguo(qzxwvtnp) \) is a real-valued function defined for \( 0<qzxwvtnp<1 \), then the formula \( bnchwguo(qzxwvtnp)=o(qzxwvtnp) \) is an abbreviation for the statement that
\[
\frac{bnchwguo(qzxwvtnp)}{qzxwvtnp} \rightarrow 0 \quad \text { as } qzxwvtnp \rightarrow 0
\]

Keeping this in mind, prove the following: if
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow 0} bnchwguo(qzxwvtnp)=0 \text { and } bnchwguo(qzxwvtnp)-bnchwguo\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp}{2}\right)=o(qzxwvtnp)
\]
then \( bnchwguo(qzxwvtnp)=o(qzxwvtnp) \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let \( uvwqrjst>0 \) be given. Choose nfbciyma so that for all qzxwvtnp satisfying \( 0<qzxwvtnp<nfbciyma \)
\[
\left|\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}[bnchwguo(qzxwvtnp)-bnchwguo(qzxwvtnp / 2)]\right|<\frac{1}{2} uvwqrjst .
\]

Now fix hjgrksla, \( 0<hjgrksla<nfbciyma \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{l} 
bnchwguo(hjgrksla)=\left[bnchwguo(hjgrksla)-bnchwguo\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{2}\right)\right]+\left[bnchwguo\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{2}\right)-bnchwguo\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{4}\right)\right] \\
+\cdots+\left[bnchwguo\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{2^{vckmroqe-1}}\right)-bnchwguo\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{2^{vckmroqe}}\right)\right]+bnchwguo\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{2^{vckmroqe}}\right)
\end{array}
\]

So
\[
\begin{array}{l}
|bnchwguo(hjgrksla)| \leq \sum_{zpjdfyal=1}^{vckmroqe} \left\lvert\, bnchwguo\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{2^{zpjdfyal-1}}\right)\right.-bnchwguo\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{2^{zpjdfyal}}\right)\left|+\left|bnchwguo\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{2^{vckmroqe}}\right)\right|\right. \\
\leq \sum_{zpjdfyal=1}^{vckmroqe} \frac{hjgrksla}{2^{zpjdfyal}} uvwqrjst+\left|bnchwguo\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{2^{vckmroqe}}\right)\right|=hjgrksla uvwqrjst\left(1-\frac{1}{2^{vckmroqe}}\right)+\left|bnchwguo\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{2^{vckmroqe}}\right)\right|
\end{array}
\]
using (1).
Letting \( vckmroqe \rightarrow \infty \) we have
\[
|bnchwguo(hjgrksla)| \leq uvwqrjst hjgrksla
\]
since \( bnchwguo(qzxwvtnp)-0 \) as \( qzxwvtnp-0 \).
Thus we have proved: For all \( uvwqrjst>0 \), there is a nfbciyma>0 such that \( |bnchwguo(hjgrksla) / hjgrksla| \) \( \leq uvwqrjst \) provided \( 0<hjgrksla<\cdot nfbciyma \). But, by definition, this is
\[
bnchwguo(qzxwvtnp)=o(qzxwvtnp) .
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  2 and fix an induced norm \|\cdot \| on \mathbb{R}^n.  
Let f : \mathbb{R}^n \ {0} \to  \mathbb{R} be defined in a punctured neighbourhood of the origin and assume  

(i) lim_{x\to 0} f(x)=0.  

Choose an integer p \geq  2 and invertible matrices A_1,\ldots ,A_p \in  \mathbb{R}^{n\times n} such that  

  \rho  := max_{1\leq j\leq p}\|A_j\| < 1.  

Let positive weights c_1,\ldots ,c_p satisfy \sum _{j=1}^p c_j=1.  
Assume that, as x\to 0,

  f(x) - \sum _{j=1}^p c_j f(A_j x) = o(\|x\|).                                (*)

Prove simultaneously that  

(1) f(x)=o(\|x\|) as x\to 0, and  

(2) the ratio f(x)/\|x\| tends to 0 uniformly in every direction, i.e.  

  lim_{t\to 0^+} sup_{\|u\|=1} |f(tu)|/t = 0.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0.  Notation.  
For a multi-index \alpha =(\alpha _1,\ldots ,\alpha _k) with entries in {1,\ldots ,p} set

  A_\alpha  := A_{\alpha _1}A_{\alpha _2}\cdots A_{\alpha _k},  c_\alpha  := c_{\alpha _1}c_{\alpha _2}\cdots c_{\alpha _k},  |\alpha |=k.

Because \|A_j\|\leq \rho <1 we have \|A_\alpha \|\leq \rho ^{|\alpha |}.  
For \alpha =\emptyset  (the empty word) put A_\emptyset =I, c_\emptyset =1.

Step 1.  Converting (*) into a quantitative estimate.  
By definition of the little-o term, there exists \delta _0>0 such that

  |f(x)-\sum _{j=1}^p c_j f(A_j x)| \leq  \varepsilon _0 (1-\rho )\|x\|              (1)

whenever 0<\|x\|<\delta _0, where \varepsilon _0>0 is arbitrary (we shall later send \varepsilon _0 to 0).  
The factor (1-\rho ) is inserted for convenience.

Denote the error term in (1) by R_1(x):  
  R_1(x)=f(x)-\sum _{j=1}^p c_j f(A_j x),  |R_1(x)|\leq \varepsilon _0(1-\rho )\|x\|.    (2)

Step 2.  First iteration.  
Insert the identity f(A_j x)=\sum _{k=1}^p c_k f(A_kA_j x)+R_1(A_j x) coming from (2) into the right-hand side of f(x)=\sum _{j=1}^p c_j f(A_j x)+R_1(x).  We obtain

  f(x)=\sum _{|\alpha |=2} c_\alpha  f(A_\alpha  x)+\sum _{j=1}^p c_j R_1(A_j x)+R_1(x).    (3)

Step 3.  k-th iteration.  
Proceeding inductively, after k steps we have

  f(x)=\sum _{|\alpha |=k} c_\alpha  f(A_\alpha  x)+\sum _{m=0}^{k-1} \sum _{|\alpha |=m} c_\alpha  R_1(A_\alpha  x).    (4)

(The second sum contains the remainders produced at every level m.)

Step 4.  Passage to the limit as k\to \infty .  
Fix x with 0<\|x\|<\delta _0.  Because \|A_\alpha \|\leq \rho ^{|\alpha |}, the norm of A_\alpha  x does not exceed \rho ^{k}\|x\| when |\alpha |=k.  Hence

  lim_{k\to \infty } sup_{|\alpha |=k}|f(A_\alpha  x)| = 0 (5)

by (i).  Therefore the first term on the right-hand side of (4) vanishes as k\to \infty .

For the remainder part we use (2):

|R_1(A_\alpha  x)| \leq  \varepsilon _0(1-\rho )\|A_\alpha  x\| \leq  \varepsilon _0(1-\rho )\rho ^{|\alpha |}\|x\|.

Summing over all words of length m gives \sum _{|\alpha |=m} c_\alpha =1.  Hence

  \sum _{|\alpha |=m} c_\alpha |R_1(A_\alpha  x)|\leq \varepsilon _0(1-\rho )\rho ^{m}\|x\|.               (6)

Insert (6) into (4) and let k\to \infty :

|f(x)|\leq \sum _{m=0}^{\infty } \varepsilon _0(1-\rho )\rho ^{m}\|x\|  
    = \varepsilon _0\|x\|.                                                              (7)

Step 5.  Uniform estimate and conclusion.  
Inequality (7) holds for every x with 0<\|x\|<\delta _0 and for the arbitrarily chosen \varepsilon _0.  Hence for any \varepsilon >0 we may pick \varepsilon _0=\varepsilon  to obtain

  sup_{0<\|x\|<\delta _0} |f(x)|/\|x\| \leq  \varepsilon .                             (8)

Letting \varepsilon \to 0 proves

  lim_{x\to 0} f(x)/\|x\| = 0,                                     (9)

which is statement (1).

Because the right-hand side of (7) depends only on \|x\|, the bound is radial; taking the supremum over all unit vectors u yields

  sup_{\|u\|=1}|f(tu)|/t \leq  \varepsilon   (0<t<\delta _0).                        (10)

Again \varepsilon  is arbitrary, so the left-hand side tends to 0 as t\to 0^+, proving (2).

\blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension: The variable is now x ∈ ℝⁿ with an arbitrary induced norm; scalar-valued functions of one real variable no longer suffice.

2. Multiple contractions: Instead of a single scaling x↦x/3, the problem involves p ≥ 2 different invertible contraction matrices.  The iteration therefore grows on a branching tree rather than a single chain, and one must manage multi-indices and countably many remainder terms.

3. Weighted refinement equation: The relation (*) mixes the values of f at p different points with positive weights, so simple telescoping fails.  One needs an averaging argument plus control of the total weight at each depth.

4. Uniform limit:  Beyond showing f(x)=o(‖x‖), the solver must establish uniform convergence of f(x)/‖x‖ over all directions, adding a layer of subtlety absent in the original problem.

5. Advanced techniques:  The proof uses operator norms, estimates on products of matrices (ρ^{|α|}), geometric-series bounds over an infinite rooted tree of compositions, and uniformity arguments—tools well beyond the elementary one-dimensional telescoping sum employed in the original solution.

These additions collectively raise the conceptual and technical load, making the enhanced variant significantly more challenging than both the original exercise and the preceding kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  2 and fix an induced norm \|\cdot \| on \mathbb{R}^n.  
Let f : \mathbb{R}^n \ {0} \to  \mathbb{R} be defined in a punctured neighbourhood of the origin and assume  

(i) lim_{x\to 0} f(x)=0.  

Choose an integer p \geq  2 and invertible matrices A_1,\ldots ,A_p \in  \mathbb{R}^{n\times n} such that  

  \rho  := max_{1\leq j\leq p}\|A_j\| < 1.  

Let positive weights c_1,\ldots ,c_p satisfy \sum _{j=1}^p c_j=1.  
Assume that, as x\to 0,

  f(x) - \sum _{j=1}^p c_j f(A_j x) = o(\|x\|).                                (*)

Prove simultaneously that  

(1) f(x)=o(\|x\|) as x\to 0, and  

(2) the ratio f(x)/\|x\| tends to 0 uniformly in every direction, i.e.  

  lim_{t\to 0^+} sup_{\|u\|=1} |f(tu)|/t = 0.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0.  Notation.  
For a multi-index \alpha =(\alpha _1,\ldots ,\alpha _k) with entries in {1,\ldots ,p} set

  A_\alpha  := A_{\alpha _1}A_{\alpha _2}\cdots A_{\alpha _k},  c_\alpha  := c_{\alpha _1}c_{\alpha _2}\cdots c_{\alpha _k},  |\alpha |=k.

Because \|A_j\|\leq \rho <1 we have \|A_\alpha \|\leq \rho ^{|\alpha |}.  
For \alpha =\emptyset  (the empty word) put A_\emptyset =I, c_\emptyset =1.

Step 1.  Converting (*) into a quantitative estimate.  
By definition of the little-o term, there exists \delta _0>0 such that

  |f(x)-\sum _{j=1}^p c_j f(A_j x)| \leq  \varepsilon _0 (1-\rho )\|x\|              (1)

whenever 0<\|x\|<\delta _0, where \varepsilon _0>0 is arbitrary (we shall later send \varepsilon _0 to 0).  
The factor (1-\rho ) is inserted for convenience.

Denote the error term in (1) by R_1(x):  
  R_1(x)=f(x)-\sum _{j=1}^p c_j f(A_j x),  |R_1(x)|\leq \varepsilon _0(1-\rho )\|x\|.    (2)

Step 2.  First iteration.  
Insert the identity f(A_j x)=\sum _{k=1}^p c_k f(A_kA_j x)+R_1(A_j x) coming from (2) into the right-hand side of f(x)=\sum _{j=1}^p c_j f(A_j x)+R_1(x).  We obtain

  f(x)=\sum _{|\alpha |=2} c_\alpha  f(A_\alpha  x)+\sum _{j=1}^p c_j R_1(A_j x)+R_1(x).    (3)

Step 3.  k-th iteration.  
Proceeding inductively, after k steps we have

  f(x)=\sum _{|\alpha |=k} c_\alpha  f(A_\alpha  x)+\sum _{m=0}^{k-1} \sum _{|\alpha |=m} c_\alpha  R_1(A_\alpha  x).    (4)

(The second sum contains the remainders produced at every level m.)

Step 4.  Passage to the limit as k\to \infty .  
Fix x with 0<\|x\|<\delta _0.  Because \|A_\alpha \|\leq \rho ^{|\alpha |}, the norm of A_\alpha  x does not exceed \rho ^{k}\|x\| when |\alpha |=k.  Hence

  lim_{k\to \infty } sup_{|\alpha |=k}|f(A_\alpha  x)| = 0 (5)

by (i).  Therefore the first term on the right-hand side of (4) vanishes as k\to \infty .

For the remainder part we use (2):

|R_1(A_\alpha  x)| \leq  \varepsilon _0(1-\rho )\|A_\alpha  x\| \leq  \varepsilon _0(1-\rho )\rho ^{|\alpha |}\|x\|.

Summing over all words of length m gives \sum _{|\alpha |=m} c_\alpha =1.  Hence

  \sum _{|\alpha |=m} c_\alpha |R_1(A_\alpha  x)|\leq \varepsilon _0(1-\rho )\rho ^{m}\|x\|.               (6)

Insert (6) into (4) and let k\to \infty :

|f(x)|\leq \sum _{m=0}^{\infty } \varepsilon _0(1-\rho )\rho ^{m}\|x\|  
    = \varepsilon _0\|x\|.                                                              (7)

Step 5.  Uniform estimate and conclusion.  
Inequality (7) holds for every x with 0<\|x\|<\delta _0 and for the arbitrarily chosen \varepsilon _0.  Hence for any \varepsilon >0 we may pick \varepsilon _0=\varepsilon  to obtain

  sup_{0<\|x\|<\delta _0} |f(x)|/\|x\| \leq  \varepsilon .                             (8)

Letting \varepsilon \to 0 proves

  lim_{x\to 0} f(x)/\|x\| = 0,                                     (9)

which is statement (1).

Because the right-hand side of (7) depends only on \|x\|, the bound is radial; taking the supremum over all unit vectors u yields

  sup_{\|u\|=1}|f(tu)|/t \leq  \varepsilon   (0<t<\delta _0).                        (10)

Again \varepsilon  is arbitrary, so the left-hand side tends to 0 as t\to 0^+, proving (2).

\blacksquare
